On shingle bank by power station outfall channel.
Feeding as the tide dropped.
Back of power station.
Initially on mud then flew west at 12:45.

Flyover flock.
Around power station channel.
Still present. The remains of the Guillemot were also still there, weirdly still pretty in tact considering it has been there at least 3 days and the number of crows/gulls/rats around.

Private site. No public access.
1 in the creek and 1 on the mill pond.
In boat yard on end of spit, always looks out of place here!.
On walk through reed bed. I used to get double figure counts a few years ago.
Private site. No public access.

Feeding in sea edge of marsh.

male. Footpath opposite B's garage.

On marsh and enjoying fresh water puddles at old power station.
male. Close to shoreline.

Being mobbed by a crow over the spit.
High tide roost, I gave up trying to count the Dunlin!.
3 posts exposed at high tide on the inland side of the spit and each one had a Tern on!.
On concrete construction in front of beach huts. Next to a Cormorant which was a nice comparison.
In trees behind power station security fence.
Single flock.
male. Calling. Private site. No public access.
High tide roost.
One was singing over the crop field behind the power station. As far as I am aware this is the first record of one singing in the Calshot area. Other records are all autumn.

02.sampleNote: Unusual to see 4 here this time of year.
Total of 11 between spit and power station.
First time more than one this winter.
4 different birds calling on walk through reed bed.
Seen from the Luttrell's Tower bit of the beach. Came in from Cadland/Lepe and purposefully made its way east parallel to the shore, at times quite close.
Fawley shore viewed from Ensign Way, Hamble. Private site. No public access.
Amongst a flock of Brent, last seen in pond opposite beach huts.
Dead on beach. Fairly sure on ID as bill details didn't fit Common nor did wing tips. This is a hard species to see live at Calshot. Another scarce for my growing deceased seabird year list...
Flew out of a tree roost in a different place to the one a few years back, I didn't know about this roost, but I have never walked this part of Ashlett pre dawn before.
Footpath opposite B's garage, unusual at Calshot.
Flew from Southampton water past castle and into Solent at 11:25.
In boat yard on end of spit.
Eating berries in car park. I mention this only because this was the only one I saw in four hours of walking. There was hundreds a couple of weeks ago.
Actually on the beach, which is unusual for cormorants/shags here. Great views of a tricky species to ID for me.
Pure white individual, haven't seen it for a few months, thought it was a gonner!.

03.sampleNote: On bank of the creek as tide came up.
Seen from Ashlett but actually over reed bed in refinery.
Singing by footpath opposite BB'S garage.
Circled briefly, my first Ashlett record.
Fawley shore....viewed from Hamble Cliff. Private site. No public access.
On mud at end of creek.
A single bird feeding in the same place every day.
Flew across from the Isle of Wight.
1 at 11:24 plus a second bird with missing primaries at 12:09. Fawley Shore viewed from Hamble Cliff. Private site. No public access.

Quite bizarre, I thought there was a piece of wood on the sea, turns out it was a female Gadwall with 13 ducklings close to the shore! Earlier in the week I saw a male on the small pond just visible on the far eastern end of Lepe, so they may have come from there. The sea was like a mill pond today so maybe the reason she decided to take them out in the sea.
Over crop field behind power station.
Calling from pond behind power station.
I regularly see 8 magpies come from the reed bed to the end of the spit and fly across Southampton water if I come down early in the morning, so assume they roost there.
Roding over footpath opposite BB'S garage. I went out specifically for Barn Owl but this was an unexpected bonus!.

Off Fawley Shore. Private site. No public access.

Remarkably first on patch since my earliest ever on 29th March.

11.sampleNote: Very tame. Hopping around RNLI boat park.
Around spit, Slav was probably there somewhere but in the fading light every Great Crested looked like a slav!.
On the pier behind the RNLI building at the same time as the Razorbill was on the water below. Definitely not a pair of species I have seen at the same time before.
Just a few m away from the Grackle on the sea adjacent to the hangar. On a normal day this would be a highlight for Calshot!.
Metal ring on right leg, couldn't read it.
In old power station channel, highest ever count for me.
Harassed one of the Marsh Harriers.

A nice surprise whilst unsuccessfully checking for the shearwater (had work yesterday unfortunately so missed it). On the water off the castle, at times very close in around the pier behind the RNLI building. Seemed fairly healthy and dived frequently. There has been a decent scarcity turn up here every week this month now.
On shingle bank on salt marsh, first time for many months. Private site. No public access.

Just a few m away from the Grackle on the sea adjacent to the hangar. On a normal day this would be a highlight for Calshot!.

Still present today, showed extremely well sitting on top of a car parked next to the castle. Seems to be very tame, unbothered by people only a few m away. I have never seen so many birders at Calshot!.
On pontoon.
On concrete construction.
Flying east. Literally thousands of finches flying east along spit. I couldn't put a number on the amount of Siskins and Goldfinches plus a few Chaffinches and Greenfinches.
Flying west.
Oddly out of place on the end of the spit.
Big flock of waders right on the end of the spit, about 50 Dunlin amongst uncountable flock of Ringed Plover.
Flying west.
Also, not seen by me, but a White Stork was in a field at Whitefield farm at 7:20 this morning.
Juvenile.
Used to be unusual here but get a bigger count every time I come here now.
By lifeboat station.
One in front of beach hut no. 60 and one past last beach hut.
Diving close to concrete construction at start of spit.
Flyover.
In stream opposite beach huts.
Amongst very large flock of Ringed Plover, Dunlin and Turnstone on shoreline. And one very soggy birdwatcher!.
Close to shoreline in front of beach hut no.1.
Mid channel adjacent castle.

A nice surprise whilst unsuccessfully checking for the shearwater (had work yesterday unfortunately so missed it). On the water off the castle, at times very close in around the pier behind the RNLI building. Seemed fairly healthy and dived frequently. There has been a decent scarcity turn up here every week this month now.
On the pier behind the RNLI building at the same time as the Razorbill was on the water below. Definitely not a pair of species I have seen at the same time before.
On beach hut no. 180.
Metal ring on right leg, couldn't read it.
One flying west along the coast and one circling the end of the spit.
Calling opposite beach huts.
Calling opposite beach huts.
Past castle.
Around spit, Slav was probably there somewhere but in the fading light every Great Crested looked like a slav!.
Past the castle.
Just offshore.
On sea in front of the the first numbered beach huts, close in. Drifted slowly west.
Flew towards Fawley Refinery.
Harassed one of the Marsh Harriers.
ad.
I think there may have been two. I saw two identical white bellied birds fly in together, one landed on the water and was the usual diver, the other didn't land and flew east.
There until around 10am when it flew off beyond the old Fawley Power station until lost to sight. Hadn't returned by the time we left at c11am.
High tide roost.
Far western end.
Adult close in along the beach.
Over the marsh.
In boat yard on end of spit, always looks out of place here!.
Diving by bridge over stream, I haven't seen one here for a few years.
Again by RNLI slipway.
The 2 adults and a juvenile still present, one feeding alongside a BHG provided a great size comparison. Almost petrel like when they dip down onto the water with their legs dangling.
Off the lifeboat station slipway, at times very close to the shore.
2 adults on shoreline in front of sailing club. There was a first winter yesterday so at least 3 in area.
On concrete construction in front of beach huts. Next to a Cormorant which was a nice comparison.

Great views of adult and juv moving up and down the shoreline. Seemingly not too bothered by people, came past very close. Also another dead Guillemot on north side of the spit, recently deceased as it still had eyes intact.
male. Close to shoreline.
1st winter flying around the pond opposite beach huts.
High tide roost, I gave up trying to count the Dunlin!.
High tide roost.
High tide roost.
3 posts exposed at high tide on the inland side of the spit and each one had a Tern on!.
Far western end.
By first toilet block.
High tide roost on southern side of spit. This species keeps on increasing here.
Diving close to concrete construction.
Not as numerous as they usually are lately. Also yesterday first GND and cormorants I have seen here in over a week, GND not here today.
Initially on mud then flew west at 12:45.
Still present. The remains of the Guillemot were also still there, weirdly still pretty in tact considering it has been there at least 3 days and the number of crows/gulls/rats around.
In pond.
Tideline corpse on north side of spit behind sailing club. A shame as never seen a live one here.
male. Not a common bird here, last year just 2 records 1 on same pond and 1 at Ashlett creek.
By lifeboat station.
Stream opposite beach huts.
All rafted together off beach huts. Also an interesting large group of Herring Gulls on the water off the castle, but nothing unusual after the strong winds overnight.
Usual bird around lifeboat station.
One both ends of beach huts.
On the end of the spit.
Feeding as the tide dropped.
Being mobbed by a crow over the spit.
Over houses.
Amongst a mobile flock of mixed waders.

Quite bizarre, I thought there was a piece of wood on the sea, turns out it was a female Gadwall with 13 ducklings close to the shore! Earlier in the week I saw a male on the small pond just visible on the far eastern end of Lepe, so they may have come from there. The sea was like a mill pond today so maybe the reason she decided to take them out in the sea.
Far western end.
w/p plummaged adult. Adult by the yellow buoys,then briefly in channel by swing bridge.
I regularly see 8 magpies come from the reed bed to the end of the spit and fly across Southampton water if I come down early in the morning, so assume they roost there.
Flew across from the Isle of Wight.
At end by lifeboat Station, very unsettled.
2 singing on one way system.
Singing on one way system.
Both sides of spit.
Far western end.
Roosting on pontoon.
2 by concrete construction.
Seen from the Luttrell's Tower bit of the beach. Came in from Cadland/Lepe and purposefully made its way east parallel to the shore, at times quite close.
Dead on beach. Fairly sure on ID as bill details didn't fit Common nor did wing tips. This is a hard species to see live at Calshot. Another scarce for my growing deceased seabird year list...
Actually on the beach, which is unusual for cormorants/shags here. Great views of a tricky species to ID for me.
In boat yard on end of spit.
At least 15 both sides of the spit.
Flying west.
Seen from end of spit. Diving constantly towards marsh bank.
Flew from Southampton water past castle and into Solent at 11:25.
Eating berries in car park. I mention this only because this was the only one I saw in four hours of walking. There was hundreds a couple of weeks ago.
Flying east.
Unusual here.
2 singing on one way system.
2 on end of spit.
One sitting on nest in fenced off area.
Flock flying west.
Flying west.
Chasing terns around ' Calshot spit ' buoy. Finally a first Hampshire record for me!.
3 males flying west.
Mixed wader flock on end of spit and pair in fenced off area, but neither sitting.
With ringed plover.
With ringed plover.
